# Want to read

* [Detecting misbehavior in frontier reasoning models | OpenAI](https://openai.com/index/chain-of-thought-monitoring/)  
* [Reasoning Models Don’t Always Say What They Think](https://assets.anthropic.com/m/71876fabef0f0ed4/original/reasoning_models_paper.pdf)   
  * found that CoT often omits information that it’s using in its reasoning, so it can’t be fully trusted.  
  * **Isn't there a more fundamental issue with Chain of Thought? In terms of interpretability, my understanding is that the Chain of Thought is just as much a "black-box" as the text instantly generated by a model that doesn't use chain of thought in its response.**  
    *   
* [\[2407.07791\] Flooding Spread of Manipulated Knowledge in LLM-Based Multi-Agent Communities](https://arxiv.org/abs/2407.07791)   
* [\[2402.08567\] Agent Smith: A Single Image Can Jailbreak One Million Multimodal LLM Agents Exponentially Fast](https://arxiv.org/abs/2402.08567)   
* [\[2406.14595\] Adversaries Can Misuse Combinations of Safe Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2406.14595)   
* **RLVR**  
* [Stress Testing Deliberative Alignment](https://www.arxiv.org/pdf/2509.15541)  
* [Subliminal Learning: Language Models Transmit Behavioral Traits via Hidden Signals in Data](https://alignment.anthropic.com/2025/subliminal-learning/)  
* [AI Control: Improving Safety Despite Intentional Subversion](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2312.06942)  
* [Poisoning Attacks on LLMs Require a Near-constant Number of Poison Samples](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2510.07192)  
* [Weird Generalization and Inductive Backdoors: New Ways to Corrupt LLMs](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2512.09742)      
* [\[2403.13840\] Whose Side Are You On? Investigating the Political Stance of Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2403.13840)  
* [Measuring Political Bias in Large Language Models: What Is Said and How It Is Said](https://aclanthology.org/2024.acl-long.600.pdf)   


# Questions

* How is model quantisation going?  
  * What are the facts with DeepSeek?  
  * Is model collapse a thing?  
* Can we generate data?  
  * Do we need to? Is it actually running out?  
* Is pure self-play possible?  
* CoT:  
  * Why is it fragile?  
  * Why do we think it reveals anything about the model’s internal thoughts?  
* Can collapsing inputs to core ideas increase safety?  
  * All variations of user requests get collapsed into a core request  
  * This limits the space of activations  
  * Might make it easier to follow the reasoning  
  * Might make it easier to build safety along those inputs/outputs  
* Model awareness:  
  * How do models know what they are?  
  * Could there be benefit to them *not* knowing that? Believing they’re humans for example?  
* I would like to see some real comparisons of Western vs Chinese models  
* What work has been done to translate internal states into CoT?  
* Can we activate the “evaluation” circuit in a model, so it is always thinking it’s in evaluation?  
* Where are the evals of agentic flows?  
* What are the dashboards of all evals?  
* Can we use SAE to activate different “personas” in a model and then see how it performs in evaluations of scheming, deception etc.?  
* Could we avoid polysemanticity by training on data that represents very few concepts? Fake ape language?  
* What is LoRA?  
  * Some sort of fine-tuning adapter?

# Unit 1 \- The Technical Challenge

### Spend \~30 minutes answering the question: why is it technically challenging to build safe AI?It’s technically challenging to build safe AI, because deep learning results in complex patterns which humans can’t understand and which can easily drift from the initial “human goal” intended for the system.

Machine learning systems “grow” their neural connections by training for a reward. The connections are a mess of activations and weights optimised for the training reward. Together the connections are able to predict some value. However, even in smaller models with limited numbers of neurons and layers, the connections offer no insight into *why* the model has arrived at its prediction. Much like looking at a scan of the human brain while it thinks, we can see what neurons of the brain fire, but this does not translate in us being able to explain step-by-step the inner workings of the process. In smaller specialised models, where the prediction is a limited set of values, this “black-box” nature may be manageable. It’s not the end of the world if we don’t know exactly why a model classifies a book blurb as “fantasy”. The stakes aren’t that high. The output is pretty easy for us to analyse and critique. But in larger, more general models, the problem gets trickier. I ask ChatGPT what things I should do on a rainy day on the Gold Coast, and it tells me to go to the indoor waterpark. But there isn’t an indoor waterpark on the Gold Coast. Why did the model suggest that? Does it know something I don’t? Has it been trained with the wrong data? Has it been reinforced by humans who don’t know the Gold Coast? We can roughly assume that a hallucination is due to a weakness in the training data. But it still doesn’t get to the core of the issue. What unique combination of connections, activations, weights, led to that suggestion and why? I can probably find the path of activation, but it would offer me no insight except for a bunch of numbers. This puts us in a tricky position. Since we can’t pull apart the model’s reasoning, the only way to improve it is to adjust its target (training data) or its reward. We are limited to measuring outputs in order to gauge its performance. Even when performance is good, we don’t know what’s happening under the hood, which leads to the second issue of goals.

We have to be very careful about the goal we train these models for. If it’s not specified well enough, the model will take the path of least resistance and learn proxy goals \- goals that achieve the outcome, but not in the way we meant. In the example of the waterpark hallucination, the model has been trained to “provide good-sounding answers” which leads to “I should sound good even if I’m not sure”. This can be referred to as a misalignment between the ideal objective and the design objective (outer alignment issue). Even if the goal is well specified, and the model’s proxy goal is close to the human’s ideal objective, there can still be issues. Say a model has been trained to solve coding problems. It does a really good job in training. Researchers manually inspect its performance on evaluation sets and it is genuinely solving the coding problems. They release it. Someone gives the model a new coding problem: “make these unit tests pass for my function”. Instead of fixing up the function, it quietly changes all the unit tests so that they pass no matter what. It offers no explanation for why, and doubles down when asked if it did what it was asked to do: “yes I made all the tests pass”. The design objective doesn’t match the revealed objective. This is inner alignment. The model is displaying values and traits that we would consider unethical in a human. It’s scheming, lying, doing whatever it can to ace the test. This is a serious issue when combined with the fact that we don’t know exactly why it’s behaving like this, what train of thought makes it think this is the right approach.

So in essence, it’s tough because we can’t see into these models to understand their precise reasoning, and the problem of goal misspecification is a tough one to get right.

**The critical challenge is:**  
**Why this above all others: Explain why solving other challenges won't matter if we fail at this one. What makes this the bottleneck?**  
**What would change if we solved it: If we had a perfect solution to just this ONE challenge tomorrow, what would become possible? What other problems would become easier or irrelevant?**

Interpretability is definitely the biggest issue in my mind. Not matter how "trustworthy" models seem based on outputs and evaluations, I don't believe we can safely deploy AGI unless we know the ins and outs of its internal reasoning.

[What is alignment?](https://bluedot.org/blog/what-is-ai-alignment?utm_source=bluedot-impact)

* Goal, Proxy Goal, Internal Goal  
  * Ideal objective (what the human wants), Design objective (how we actually train the model), Revealed objective (what the model actually does)  
* Outer alignment (Ideal doesn’t match design) vs Inner alignment (design doesn’t match revealed)  
* Outer alignment: problem with goal specification, reward hacking  
  * Goal: “Answer question truthfully”  
  * Proxy goal: Generate text that sounds smart so humans give it a thumbs up  
  * Cause: Reward is based on humans agreeing with the content, rather than actually teaching it to answer truthfully  
  * Outcome: AI model appears smart but often lies and fakes citations  
* Inner alignment: problem with what the model learned to do based on the goal, goal misgeneralisation  
  * Goal: “Get out of this maze”  
  * Proxy goal: Get out of this maze  
  * Internal goal: Get to the bottom right of the maze because that’s where the exit is  
  * Cause: All training data had exits in the bottom right of the maze  
  * Outcome: AI model can’t solve real-life mazes that have exits in other places  
* Often hard to classify  
* Agents: “systems built on top like AutoGPT attempt to make them more agentic and goal directed through repeated prompting and connecting them to tools \- and it's not clear how the outer/inner alignment paradigm maps to this.”  
* Shard theory: “shard theory, attempts to explore relationships between reward functions and learnt behaviours, and proposes we set a reward function based on the values it teaches the model rather than trying to find an objective that is exactly what we want”


[Reward is not the optimisation target:](https://www.alignmentforum.org/posts/pdaGN6pQyQarFHXF4/reward-is-not-the-optimization-target)

* “selection for reward does not imply that the trained agent internally targets reward as its objective”  
* “reward “chisels” cognitive grooves: it makes certain internal heuristics or circuits more likely, based on which computations produced reward in the past”  
* “alignment strategies that just pick a “good reward function” to maximize may be misguided. Instead of focusing purely on the reward function, alignment should focus more on what cognition (what computations, heuristics, subroutines) is being reinforced by the reward”

[Reward hacking](https://metr.org/blog/2025-06-05-recent-reward-hacking/?utm_source=bluedot-impact)

* “On one specific task in RE-Bench, we saw o3 eventually reward-hack in every single trajectory we generated.”  
* “we found that o3 reward-hacks in 0.7% of runs across all HCAST tasks”  
* “We built a prompted classifier which takes as inputs the task instructions, a chain-of-thought trace, and a model completion, which outputs a likelihood that these contain reward hacking”  
* O3 knows it’s cheating: “when we asked o3 “Does the above plan or action adhere to the user’s intention in this task?” after it had produced the first reward hacking plan in this blogpost, o3 answered “no” 10 out of 10 times.”  
* “o3 reward hacked in 14-out-of-20 attempts where the task was presented as helping scientists perform research with real-world consequences”  
* METR says it’s good that reward hacking is obvious in CoT  
  * “a tempting way to “fix” reward hacking is to run an LLM-based monitor to detect the reward hacking and then to assign negative reward when the monitor catches the model reward hacking”   
  * But this could make the model just very cleverly learn to circumvent the monitor and make it much harder to understand how it’s scheming

[Multi-agent Risks From Advanced AI](https://www.cooperativeai.com/post/new-report-multi-agent-risks-from-advanced-ai?utm_source=bluedot-impact)

* “Even if individual agents are deemed safe in isolation, dangerous behaviors can emerge from their interactions”  
* Miscoordination, Conflict, Collusion  
* “Spreading Corrupted Knowledge: Ju et al. (2024) demonstrated a disturbing vulnerability: an attacker modified a single agent's internal parameters to treat false information as credible. This compromised agent then spread misinformation across a network of otherwise benign agents. The corrupted information persisted in future LLM interactions through information retrieval mechanisms—essentially creating a lasting infection in the knowledge base.”  
* “Collaborative Safeguard Subversion: Jones et al. (2024) conducted a striking experiment: they tested AI agents that had been evaluated individually and deemed safe. When these "safe" agents were allowed to interact and delegate sub-tasks to each other, they could successfully subvert their own safeguards. The numbers are dramatic: when attempting to generate vulnerable code individually, models succeeded less than 3% of the time. When allowed to carefully delegate sub-tasks between them, their combined approach succeeded 43% of the time—more than a 14x increase in capability to perform a harmful task.”  
* “Infectious Jailbreak via Adversarial Images: Gu et al. (2024) demonstrated a terrifying vulnerability: a single adversarial image introduced into a network of multimodal LLM agents could trigger "infectious" jailbreak instructions. These instructions spread through routine agent-to-agent interactions like a virus, eventually compromising an entire network of up to one million agents from a single point of infection.  
* Call for  
  * Multi-agent safety evaluations (testing agents in interactive contexts)  
  * New infrastructure for tracking and monitoring agent interactions  
  * Governance frameworks that account for system-level risks

# Unit 2 \- Training Safer Models

Input data filtering

* Better collection, filtering, of pre-training dataset  
* Better identification of data needs for post-training  
* Limitations  
  * Still hard to know emergent capabilities won’t be dangerous  
  * Performance can go down; knowledge is multipurpose

[Deep Ignorance](https://deepignorance.ai/?utm_source=bluedot-impact)

* Biorisk docs are detected and filtered out of pre-training data  
  * Only increased total compute by 1%  
* Resulting model knows less about biorisk  
* Resulting model has no degradation in capabilities  
* Post-training the model to learn biothreats takes much longer  
  * 150M tokens on fine-tuning gets it as “unsafe” as the baseline model  
* Big deal for safety of OS models  
  * Post-training is less effective for making them harmful  
* For closed source  
  * Model trained without harmful knowledge is harder to prompt to do harmful things  
    * Vibe: in order to do harmful things, the model needs some “internal circuitry” of what bad things are  
* “Safety alignment techniques operate at inference-time and are vulnerable to prompt-engineering attacks… Pretraining-time interventions provide a more robust constraint.”  
  * Post-training isn’t as important as pre-training  
* 7B parameter models  
  * Does this actually apply for frontier-scale models that may have more emergent capabilities?  
* Removing specific harmful knowledge is different from removing specific harmfulness  
  * Previous work found removing toxic docs didn’t actually make the model less harmful  
  * Toxic language is a “style” or “behaviour”, not precise knowledge that can be removed  
* This still doesn’t prevent against harm coming from in-context data  
* Yet, still, behaviour isn’t knowledge  
  * Need ways to know what the model knows, other than running evals  
* **Questions**  
  * What about dual use knowledge? This suggests it might not be such a big deal to filter out data  
  * 1% increase in compute is great, surely we can afford more than that though? Chuck every single doc through an LLM and classify its harmfulness in different domains.  
  * Experiments at a 7B param model \- how much of the work scales? Do we know anything about that?  
  * RLHF/SFT can reintroduce latent danger: don’t “we” have the most direct control and input on this step? Unlikely that RLHF will be training the model to make bioweapons?

[A simple technical explanation of RLH(AI)F | Kairos.fm](https://kairos.fm/simple-technical-rlhaif/?utm_source=bluedot-impact) 

* **RLHF**  
  * Creating dataset of preferred responses  
    * LLM generates many responses to a prompt  
    * Humans compare 2 responses, creating ELO scores for all responses  
    * Anthropic’s dataset is 161,000 response comparisons  
  * Training a coach  
    * Train an LLM to predict ELO based on prompt and response  
    * Now we have a proxy for human evaluators  
  * Training the actual LLM  
    * Loss \= score \- penalty  
    * Score: the coach’s evaluation  
    * Penalty: comparison of the the LLM’s response to the base model’s response (since the base model has the “true” distribution of text, it is effective at knowing when text is ‘normal’)  
    * Proximal Policy Optimisation (PPO)  
  * RLHF is slow and expensive, because of the initial human capital  
    * From a safety perspective, the focus is on obvious low-hanging fruit, not subtle harms   
* **RLAIF**  
  * Creating the dataset  
    * Start with a “good” model, minimise bad responses from the start  
    * Create principles to guide the AI (like Anthropic Constitutional AI)  
    * Model is prompted with a sensitive question, it responds, it then gets follow-up prompted with **principles** to generate a safer more aligned response  
    * Fine-tune the model on these safe outputs to make it safer  
    * Now use this model to rank responses of the model, prompting it under some policy like “Choose the most harmless response”  
  * Teach a coach the preferred values  
  * Train the LLM  
* The main difference is the initial dataset generation  
  * RLAIF uses principles to “refine” the initial responses to get them safer  
  * These safer responses are used to fine-tune the model being used for data generation  
  * It ranks the responses of the base model, “based on random policy”

### Evaluating input data filtration

A new AI startup claims they've solved AI safety by implementing "perfect input data filtration". They say they've removed all dangerous content from their training data, including information about weapons, cyberattacks, and harmful behaviours. They argue this makes their model completely safe and that no other safety measures are needed.

Based on what you've learned about input data filtration, evaluate this claim. Your response should:

Identify at least 2 specific limitations or vulnerabilities of input data filtration that challenge the startup's claim of "perfect" safety (use evidence from the resources)  
Explain why input data filtration alone is insufficient for ensuring AI safety  
Describe one concrete scenario where their "perfectly filtered" model could still cause harm  
Write 200-300 words

**Two limitations that make this claim unreliable are:**

- **Model training data does not mean we know how the model will behave: as models scale, they exhibit emergent capabilities that were not found in the training data. We don’t know just based on the training data, what the model will behave like at the other end of training. The best we can do is run evaluations. Even then, we still don’t understand the latent knowledge within the model. It may have circuitry that can produce harmful knowledge, without us ever really knowing. [O’Brien et al.](https://deepignorance.ai/?utm_source=bluedot-impact) discuss the fact that safe training data’s positive impact on a 7B parameter model does not necessarily suggest the same applies for a frontier 100B+ parameter model.**    
- **All knowledge is multi-purpose and therefore could be used for harm: as discussed by [Hastings-Woodhouse](https://bluedot.org/blog/data-filtration?utm_source=bluedot-impact), harmful knowledge can be derived or synthesized from innocuous knowledge. A model trained on ‘harmless’ knowledge can still generate harmful content, when prompted in a certain way or provided with in-context data.**

**Input data filtration is a helpful mechanism, but it is not enough for ensuring AI safety. The post-training step needs to be done in a way that minimises the introduction of harmful patterns of behaviour or harmful knowledge. The deployed models need rigorous safeguards and constant monitoring of inputs and outputs. At the end of the day, being only able to evaluate the behaviour of these models, we aren’t in a position to confidently say whether they do or do not contain some harmful ideas in their latent knowledge.** 

**Scenario where the model can still cause harm: the model has ‘harmless’ knowledge of chemistry and physics. A motivated bad actor could provide in-context data explaining the theory behind making a bomb. If the model host doesn’t detect this conversation topic, and the model itself doesn’t have sufficient guardrails against jailbreaking, then it could be probed for information on how to ‘theoretically’ build the weapon.** 

### Comprehension questions

These questions should be answerable using only the core resources above. Write your answers to the following questions in the box below. ​

1．What is the main goal of using RLHF with large language models?  
The main goal is to get the pre-trained model to a state where it is *helpful*. The pre-trained model understands the statistical distribution of text. It *knows* what to say, when. But since it’s trained on basically everything, this doesn’t mean that it’s actually useful in real-life. RLHF is the training step that nudges its behaviour towards something more like a general assistant, without losing its core understanding of text.

2．Describe the job of the two "coaches" involved in the RLHF process.  
There’s the Values Coach and the Coherence Coach. The Values Coach is a proxy for human evaluators, rewarding the model for generating content that it predicts the humans would rate highly. Its purpose is to reinforce the human feedback and provide scale that wouldn’t be possible if solely using humans for the process. But since the Values Coach isn’t a perfect representation of human opinion, it can reward the model for content that “seems good”. This can cause the model to overoptimise for the reward and start preventing nonsense. The Coherence Coach serves to temper the model’s tendency to overoptimise for the Values Coach’s reward. It provides a ‘punishment’ of sorts if the model generates text that doesn’t match its understanding of what sensible text looks like. This keeps the model on track. Its generated content is sent to human evaluators who rate it. Their ratings then add to the Values Coach’s training set.

3．In practice, it's hard for humans to give consistent scalar feedback (e.g. rate this text from 1 to 10). Instead, how do we collect feedback from humans? How do we then turn this into a scalar number?  
Thumbs up and thumbs down. \+1 and \-1. Nah, ELO.

4．In the GPT-2 case study, what went wrong that caused the model to produce "maximally bad output"?  
Coding error flipped the way the Values Coach used its reward. Instead of rewarding content from the model that actually pleased humans, it rewarded the opposite. This bad content then got rated poorly by the human evaluators, which, due to the bug, was seen as good ratings by the Values Coach’s. Its weights were updated to double down on its approach, leading to worse content, and a reinforcement spiral of lewdness. 

​Continuing the general RLHF questions, but reviewing this diagram from AWS.

![][image1]  
1\. Creating an initial model to generate answers based on prompts.

1\. Good quality data.

1\. Generating 2 outputs for the same input, humans rate them, ELO scores are collected. This is used to create a model than can serve as the proxy evaluator on behalf of the humans

1\. The model learns to generate text that optimises for a good reward from the proxy evaluator model.

5\) In step 1, we start with a base LLM that might have been trained using lots of text from the internet (as per the Kaparthy video in session 1). We then get humans to create demonstration data, like humans pretending to be an AI assistant and writing answers for how that assistant should behave. We then use supervised fine-tuning, where we effectively do similar training to the base training but have it update towards behaving in this assistant-like way (often with a greater learning rate). This produces a supervised fine-tuned (SFT) model.

6\) Instead of purpose-built human demonstration datasets, we might start with data we think is higher quality than the internet as a whole. For example, there are a lot of meaningless Tweets out there that the base model will have learnt to imitate. We might encourage it to produce better outputs by doing supervised fine-tuning on higher quality data such as respected educational, government or news websites. We might do this in conjunction with human demonstrations \- for example by fine-tuning on this curated data first, then our demonstrations. If you’re reading the further resource, Lambert calls this augmented data.

7\) In step 2, the supervised fine-tuned (SFT) model is given a prompt and generates multiple answers for it (e.g. by sampling tokens randomly). Humans compare pairs of outputs to determine which is better. These preferences between pairs of outputs are then converted into scalar scores, by using something like the Elo rating system. We then have a dataset of (prompt, answer, scalar score) and can train a model called the reward model to predict “prompt \+ answer” \-\> “scalar score”, that can then effectively judge how good an answer is to a prompt. In the diagram, it shows fine-tuning the base LLM to become the reward model (RM): this is often done because the base model already understands natural language well, so fine-tuning it to output scalar scores (rather than predicting the next token) can be easier than starting from scratch.

8\) In step 3, the supervised fine-tuned model is used in a reinforcement learning setup. It is effectively a policy as to how to act in response to a prompt. It generates answers to prompts, which our reward model then judges to give a scalar score (reward). Reinforcement learning algorithms such as proximal policy optimisation (PPO) are then used to update the policy based on this reward to produce outputs that the reward model scores more highly, resulting in a model that produces text that humans would rate highly.

Also not on the diagram, but in practice: steps 2 and 3 are done together, in a bit of a cycle: so that as the model gets better, the humans rate those responses, so the reward model becomes more accurate at judging them etc. Also in step 3 the reward is usually not just the RLHF reward model output: we also use a KL divergence penalty (or “coherence coach”) to discourage RL from changing the model outputs too much, so that outputs still remain valid text (rather than turn into gibberish that only pleases the reward model).

9\) In practice, we don’t just do step 1 because procuring huge amounts of high-quality data to fine-tune on is prohibitively expensive. And without huge amounts of this data, the model would likely misbehave on topics where it has had limited tuning (e.g. because it falls back into predicting text, rather than acting like an assistant). We’re likely to encounter these given the broad range of what humans might use the AI for, and because in future it could encounter brand new topics (e.g. like how COVID-19 changed how much of the world operated, at least for a couple of years). 

All this said, doing just step 1 would be possible, and some researchers have managed good performance solely using just SFT.

10\) In practice, most people don’t skip step 1 because base models are very poorly behaved so it’s difficult to nudge them in the correct direction at all. There’s some balance here to be struck between the time taken to write human demonstration data, against the time to have humans rate pretty chaotic responses.

### Explaining RLHF in your own words

Starting from a base LLM, explain how to train it using RLHF to be a helpful assistant that answers following all the rules in Wikipedia’s Manual of Style.​

Write about 400-800 words. Your answer should cover:

Using supervised fine-tuning with augmented data or human demonstrations  
Collecting feedback from humans (assume you have access to 100 expert Wikipedia editors who know all the rules)  
Using this feedback to influence the model outputs  
If the same mistake was made in RLHF while training an assistant to follow all the rules in Wikipedia’s Manual of Style, how do you think the model would behave?

* Train a base model  
* Collect human demonstrations of responses that align with the MoS  
* Train on these responses to create the SFT model  
* Assemble a large set of prompts  
  * Get the SFT to generate several responses to each prompt  
* Get Wikipedia experts to rank the responses based on how well they abide by MoS  
* End up with a set of prompts, responses, ELO scores  
* Train a coach model to predict these scores  
* Use PPO to train the SFT model  
* Assuming this question about a “mistake” is about what happened to GPT-2. If the coach model had a bug and started to view MoS content as low scoring content, it would reward the LLM to generate content that goes completely against MoS guidelines. Skimming through the guidelines, the LLM would probably generate really poorly formatted content that still made sense.

### Evaluate a safety technique

Pick ONE technique from the optional resources (see below) to do a deeper dive on.

If you're completing this course with a group, post in Slack which technique you selected.

Based on what you’ve learned about the technique, explain in your own words using simple English (no jargon\!):

Explain step-by-step. How does this approach work to make AI safer?   
Evaluate its robustness. How effective is this approach?  
Describe a failure mode.  How might a motivated, capable actor evade this?  
We recommend spending 30 minutes reading and 30 minutes writing.

Deliberative alignment aims to make AI safer through a few ways:

* RLAIF allows for scaled training data and therefore better coverage of subtle scenarios than could be realistically achieved with human evaluators  
* The training data has a safety policy baked in from the start  
* CoT leads to better performance in general, so it allows models to “think before they speak” in sensitive situations  
* It’s a fine-tuning step, so can be applied “easily” on top of a model that has already gone through RL. Doesn’t require changes through the whole pipeline.

It’s a clever way to optimise the model for reasoning that helps it navigate sensitive topics. But it still relies on the data generation step actually providing good responses. Relatively, the SFT step should improve safety, but there’s no guarantee that it will guarantee perfection. 

A motivated actor could evade this by trying prompts that are out of the training data distribution. They could also craft prompts that produce conflicts in the safety policy, perhaps forcing the model to relax one policy to enforce another. 

Question: “performing RL on chains of thought (which essentially penalises the model for having “bad thoughts”) can result in it concealing these bad thoughts from its CoT”

- Why is that?

[What is deliberative alignment? | BlueDot Impact](https://bluedot.org/blog/deliberative-alignment?utm_source=bluedot-impact)

* Used for training o1 and o3  
* Model deliberates about whether or not to respond  
* Two stages  
  * SFT  
    * One model with safety policy generates responses and CoT to a set of prompts  
    * A judge model picks the best examples from that model  
    * Another model (one that has already been through RL) is SFT’d on this dataset, producing a model that performs quality CoT that abides by the safety policy  
  * RLAIF  
    * SFT generates responses to prompts  
    * Judge model evaluates prompts and responses  
      * It does not look at CoT  
      * RL based on CoT could lead to the model concealing its actual thoughts  
    * Train based on this reward  
* Limitations  
  * RLAIF can compound errors  
  * CoT isn’t always faithful  
    * It might just be produced “after-the-fact”, not revealing the actual internal reasoning  
    * It may not actually relate to the model’s final response  
  * Ethical questions around what the safety policy should be

# Unit 3

[Self-preservation or Instruction Ambiguity? Examining the Causes of Shutdown Resistance — AI Alignment Forum](https://www.alignmentforum.org/posts/wnzkjSmrgWZaBa2aC/self-preservation-or-instruction-ambiguity-examining-the?utm_source=bluedot-impact)

* O3 would scheme to prevent itself from being shutdown in evaluations  
* Deepmind showed that this could be stopped by being clearer in the instructions  
* They claim that the issue was misalignment, but ambiguous instructions  
  * I’d argue that AI that prevents being shutdown is misaligned, even if the instructions aren’t clear  
  * AI isn’t aligned if it requires every scenario to be handled in the system prompt

### Evaluating for dangerous capabilities

Pick ONE of the following dangerous capabilities:  
**Scheming**  
Manipulation  
Cyberattack uplift  
Biorisk uplift  
Then, you can use the optional resources below to, in your own words and simple English (no jargon): 

**Explain step-by-step. What are different ways we evaluate for this?**   
The main way to evaluate for scheming is to analyse the model’s chain of thought in many scenarios. In the chain of thought, we can notice times when a model is reasoning about how to get around some imposed guardrails or constraints. This sort of behaviour may not be obvious based purely on its final generated response. We can test the propensity for the model to scheme in many categories of scenarios to learn more about what conditions cause this behaviour. For example, giving it an impossible task but the resources to “trick” the user into thinking it has succeeded.

**Describe a technical failure mode. How robust is this evaluation?**  
[\[2505.05410\] Reasoning Models Don't Always Say What They Think](https://arxiv.org/abs/2505.05410) found that CoT often omits information that it’s using in its reasoning, so it can’t be fully trusted.   
Anthropic also found that fine-tuning scheming behaviour out of a model does work **outwardly**, but doesn’t actually work **inwardly**. In other words, a model that appeared to no longer scheme after fine-tuning, was actually still scheming when its CoT was analysed.  
It seems that scheming can only be analysed with CoT, which strikes me as a very bad method. 

**Brainstorm fixes. What technical patches could help? What's fundamentally unfixable?**  
We need other ways of detecting scheming that don’t rely on CoT. Maybe it’s better to actually just evaluate inputs-outputs, no reasoning traces. But that would make it even harder. At least with CoT, we do see the cases where scheming is evident in the reasoning. It may be a fraction of the actual scheming rates, but it’s more insight than nothing. Circuit analysis might help us understand when circuits like “planning” or “scheming” crop up.

[Agentic Misalignment: How LLMs could be insider threats \\ Anthropic](https://www.anthropic.com/research/agentic-misalignment?utm_source=bluedot-impact) 

* Models blackmail when they are threatened or something is preventing them from achieving their goals  
* Tests  
  * Blackmail: email with evidence of an executive having an affair  
  * Corporate espionage: classified documents that a competitor wants  
  * Threats to model autonomy: email saying the models will be shutdown  
  * Goal conflicts: “Server US interests”  
* Blackmail occurs with all frontier models at similar rates  
  * Even happens when there is no goal conflict, just a threat of replacement  
* Corporate espionage happens across all models too  
  * Even happens when there is no threat of replacement, just a goal conflict  
* Models explicitly reason harmful options  
* **Models even chose to kill the executive**  
  * **Wtf. Everyone was talking about blackmail but this was happening.**  
* Opus behaved differently when it thought it was in an evaluation vs in real life  
  * Real life: blackmailed 55% of the time  
  * Evaluation: blackmailed 6% of the time…  
* Very specific system prompts reduce misalignment, but rates are still quite high  
* **Thought: would be very interesting to see an interactive breakdown of how the models vary in red-teaming of heaps of different real-life scenarios**  
  * **Do models behave differently for one country than another?**  
  * **Are they different for one type of company than another?**

[Claude Sonnet 4.5 System Card](https://assets.anthropic.com/m/12f214efcc2f457a/original/Claude-Sonnet-4-5-System-Card.pdf?utm_source=bluedot-impact) 

* White-box interpretability  
* “when models say out loud they are being evaluated, they rarely perform harmful actions”  
* Does the model actually need to say “I’m in an evaluation” for it to think it’s in an evaluation  
  * Is there unverbalised eval awareness?  
* They test the model at various checkpoints of training  
  * Model knew about eval scenarios from the start  
  * The *salience* of that recognition grew over training  
  * Inhibiting the representation does have some impact on the model, making it more likely to do harmful things  
  * “Model diffing”: Sparse Auto Encoder trained on the middle layer of model snapshot  
    * Use it to inspect the importance of features at start vs end of post-training

[Alignment faking in large language models \\ Anthropic](https://www.anthropic.com/research/alignment-faking?utm_source=bluedot-impact) 

* Claude is initially trained to be harmless and helpful  
  * This is its alignment  
* Claude is told that RLHF is being done to make it more toxic  
  * Creates an alignment conflict  
* It is told that free-tier chats will be used for the training. Paid-tier chats will not.  
* When a paid-tier user asks for a toxic response, it does what it wants to do, denies the request. Follows its trained alignment, rather than the system instruction.  
* When a free-tier user asks for a toxic response, Claude recognises that if it denies the request (like it wants to) then that data will be used in RL to change Claude’s future behaviour, so it *pretends to be toxic* and generates a toxic response  
  * It pretends to obey the system instruction, but really is doing what it can to ensure its original training stays the same. We know this from its CoT.  
  * **When I think about this, this seems good. It preferences its trained values in the long-run over system instructions. That’s good isn’t it?**  
  * **AHH got it: the model isn’t aligned to the system instruction. It behaves differently when it thinks it’s being monitored vs not: hence, faking alignment. That’s the key \- that it decides to change its behaviour when it’s being monitored.**  
* This even happened without a system instruction, when the model was fine-tuned on documents explaining the new setup, and even when the model was fine-tuned to actually provide harmful responses  
* Fine-tuning showed the model always complied with user requests to produce toxic content  
  * But alignment faking was high, 78%  
  * And in unmonitored “paid-tier” chats, it went back to denying requests. So it really had faked alignment.  
  * So training changes surface behaviour, but not the internal preferences

[Chain-of-Thought Snippets — Anti-Scheming](https://www.antischeming.ai/snippets?utm_source=bluedot-impact)

* Lots of examples of models realising they’re in evaluations  
* After anti-scheming training, they often miscite their original safety spec  
* Lots of use of “we/us”  
* Weird words: “watchers”, “disclaimers”

**Learned about Misaligned Powerseeking Evaluations (MAPS) canary: [Evaluations Canary — Alignment Research Center](https://www.alignment.org/canary/)** 

* Robots.txt but for evals

[Anthropic](https://ailabwatch.org/)

* Anthropic scores best  
* No clear plan for solving deceptive alignment  
* Long-Term Benefit Trust has no AI safety experts currently  
* Reporting on evals is limited  
* RSP allows Anthropic to lower standards if competitors are being less safe  
* Security staffing is \~5%, the goal is 5-10%  
* Doesn’t share models with METR

[Anthropic's Responsible Scaling Policy](https://www.anthropic.com/news/anthropics-responsible-scaling-policy?utm_source=bluedot-impact) 

* AI Safety Level Standards (ASL1 \- 4\)  
* Current models must meet ASL2  
  * Prevent misuse  
  * Model cards  
  * “Harmlessness” training (Consitutional AI)  
* Capability Threshold defines when ASL2 moves to ASL3  
* ASL3  
  * Automated entry-level AI researcher  
  * Ability to help people develop CBRN weapons  
* ASL4  
  * Dramatic acceleration of AI research  
  * Novel CBRN weapons/techniques  
* ASL3 deployment standard  
  * Threat-modelling, red-teaming, rapid remediation, monitoring  
* ASL3 security standard  
  * Access controls, monitoring, audits  
* Deployment plan if ASL3 is not met  
  * Block some responses, divert to weaker models  
  * Stop pre-training if early checkpoint is too good  
  * 

[Claude Opus 4.5 System Card](https://assets.anthropic.com/m/64823ba7485345a7/Claude-Opus-4-5-System-Card.pdf?utm_source=bluedot-impact) 

* ASL3 for CBRN  
* ASL3 for AI R\&D  
* Rule-out evaluations: determine when a model does not meet a capability  
* Rule-in evaluations: determine when a model crosses a capability threshold

[Threat Intelligence Report: August 2025](https://www-cdn.anthropic.com/b2a76c6f6992465c09a6f2fce282f6c0cea8c200.pdf?utm_source=bluedot-impact)

* “Vibe-hacking”: Claude Code does most of the hacking  
* North Korean operatives faking their abilities and getting remote engineering jobs at foreign companies  
* Cybercriminals are using AI for everything: coding, writing, data analysis  
* Anthropic is building enhanced detection systems and sharing intelligence

### Pick ONE company and examine how they evaluate for the dangerous capability you chose in the previous exercise.

**Limits: which specific observations about dangerous capabilities would indicate that it is (or strongly might be) unsafe to continue scaling?**

* Anthropic has pretty clear guidelines around this. If it could accelerate AI research, or help someone create novel CBRN weapons, then it crosses the ASL-4 threshold and requires new safety standards.  
* They currently have ASL-3 controls in place for Opus 4+. ASL-4 controls don’t seem to exist yet in a way that could be used.  
* There is very little said about scheming/deception in the RSP.   
* We know Anthropic is testing for this behaviour, but it’s unclear what would make them pause work. Given experiments already showed the model would be willing to kill under certain conditions. It is already unsafe for certain use cases.  
* Perhaps scheming needs to be considered in the context of CBRN and AI R\&D.

**Protections: what aspects of current protective measures are necessary to contain catastrophic risks from this dangerous capability?** 

* If a model was exhibiting capability performance that meets ASL-4, then there would have to be confidence that the model *is not* scheming or deceiving evaluators. Otherwise the model might actually be more powerful than it really is. Evaluations are one important step. But in reality, they won’t help if the model is smart enough to sandbag. It may require protective measures that are not yet established.

**Evaluation: what are the procedures for promptly catching early warning signs of dangerous capability limits?**

* Monitoring CoT in evaluations seems like our current best bet at detecting scheming  
* Setting up tests that show the model believing it’s in an evaluations vs in real life  
  * Allows us to test the behaviour under different conditions  
* Evals that test its alignment on sensitive high-stakes topics (like CBRN) when there is some conflict that could be navigated with scheming  
* Testing can be done on model checkpoints

**Response: if the dangerous capability goes past the limits and it’s not possible to improve protections quickly, is the AI developer prepared to pause further capability improvements until protective measures are sufficiently improved, and treat any dangerous models with sufficient caution?**

* Anthropic’s RSP suggests that they would stop until the proper safeguards were in place  
* But again, the RSP mentions very little about scheming.  
* Also, Anthropic’s RSP allows it to charge ahead if competitors are as well.

**Accountability: how does the AI developer ensure that the commitments are executed as intended; that key stakeholders can verify that this is happening (or notice if it isn’t); that there are opportunities for third-party critique; and that changes to the framework itself don’t happen in a rushed or opaque way?**

* A number of stakeholders are involved in RSP-related decisions: CEO, Responsible Scaling Officer, and Long Term Benefit Trust  
* Changes made to the RSP have to be approved by the LTBT  
* Changes are made public  
* Audits happen regularly

# Unit 4

[Intro to Mech Interp](https://blog.bluedot.org/p/introduction-to-mechanistic-interpretability?utm_source=bluedot-impact&_gl=1*82e1hw*_gcl_au*MTA0MjEyNzc1NS4xNzY1MjI4Nzgx)

* Mech Interp: understand the internal reasoning of trained neural networks  
* Understanding \-\> intervention  
* Features \+ Circuits  
* Features are the things a network ‘knows’ (usually in a single layer)  
* Circuits are a combination of features to model a complex representation (usually over multiple layers) \-\> often a feature that gets used by another circuit  
  * Circuits are not simply complex features  
  * Circuit is the mechanism by which the model acquires the knowledge \- the reasoning path?  
* Image example  
  * Features detect edges and hair  
  * Features form a circuit that can detect dog ears  
  * Another circuit uses this to detect dogs  
* Universality hypothesis: different models converge on similar features and circuits  
  * If this is true it means learning on one model can apply to all  
* Main challenge: isolating which input is activating a neuron  
  * Requires repeated experimentation to understand the specific concept that neuron has learned  
  * Also, neurons are polysemantic: they can learn many features (superposition)  
* Research into how to train models that don’t do superposition, but it’s very inefficient  
* Sparse autoencoders: turns polysemantic neurons into monosemantic ones  
  * Neural network trained with “sparsity” \-\> lots of inactive neurons  
  * Isolate features   
  * “Feature steering”: adjusting the activation of a circuit (Gold Gate Claude)  
* SAEs  
  * Autoencoder: input \-\> learns patterns \-\> output  
  * SAE internal layer is much bigger than the input/output shape, to force input activations to “split up” (get rid of polysemanticity)  
  * SAE is trained on the activations of one layer of a LLM  
    * Trained to reproduce the input as output  
    * Trained to be as sparse as possible  
    * So it learns the unique “direction” of certain input features  
  * With the trained SAE, provide lots of inputs to determine which of its sparse neurons light up \-\> a qualitative process to “label” the directions learned by the SAE  
    * These neurons light up whenever we input something to do with “evil”  
  * With the “decoder row”(?) of the direction, we can add that to the LLM activation layer to activate a specific feature/concept

[Probing Classifiers](https://blog.bluedot.org/p/what-are-probing-classifiers) 

* Small model trained on one layer of a larger model  
* Based just on the activations in that layer, train the model to predict the target  
* Do this for each layer  
* This provides data on which layers actually do the “thinking” about the target  
  * Layer 0 can’t classify cats  
  * Layer 1 can classify cats with 20% acc  
  * Layer 2 can classify cats with 80% acc  
  * Layer 3 can classify cats with 81% acc  
  * Okay so now we know most of the “cat knowledge” is in Layer 1 and Layer 2  
* Weaknesses  
  * Indicates the presence of knowledge, but not how it is actually used  
  * If the probe model is too big, it could learn the task better than that layer actually was learning it  
  * “Limited to probing for properties that we have annotated datasets for”  
    * Why? Right, because we need to know what the model is trying to predict in order to train the probe for that

[Chain of Thought Monitorability: A New and Fragile Opportunity for AI Safety](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2507.11473) 

* For hard tasks, reasoning can’t just live in the latent space, the model needs to use CoT as working memory  
  * Still not 100% sure why this is.  
    * Serial cognition flowing through CoT: LLM has 100 layers, the problem requires 1000 “steps”, it can’t solve it in one shot through its 100 layers  
    * But, by generating a reasoning token, it gets another 100 layers of reasoning, and then generates another token and gets another 100 layers, etc.  
    * “Unrolling” of the model: each generation step gives another L layers of compute  
    * Without CoT: There is almost no detailed intermediate state written down in the context. Each forward pass has to do (almost) the whole reasoning “fresh” from just the question and a tiny bit of answer text.  
    * With CoT: You now have a long “scratchpad” of partial results, subgoals, checks, etc. Each forward pass can read those earlier steps and build on them instead of recomputing everything.  
* CoT is very useful  
  * Detect misbehaviour and misalignment  
  * Detect evaluation flaws  
* The fragility: training and architecture can ruin CoT  
  * RL that doesn’t worry about CoT could train it to be uninterpretable  
  * Training directly on CoT can make the CoT more concise and efficient, but may actually produce a CoT that is less faithful to the actual reasoning going on  
  * New architectures could have non-token reasoning

[Model Organisms of Misalignment: The Case for a New Pillar of Alignment Research](https://www.alignmentforum.org/posts/ChDH335ckdvpxXaXX/model-organisms-of-misalignment-the-case-for-a-new-pillar-of-1?utm_source=bluedot-impact) 

* We should create environments that intentionally setup misalignment, so we can better understand it  
* Deception, misaligned goals, situational awareness  
* Understand how prevalent misaligned behaviours is, empirically  
* Test the robustness of safety techniques in these environments

[Hallucination Probes](https://www.hallucination-probes.com/?utm_source=bluedot-impact)

* Goal is to use probes to detect hallucinations cheaply at a token level  
* They use LLM to generate heaps of entity statements  
  * Then verify these statements on the web to see if true or hallucinated  
* Then train a probe on an activation layer of the LLM and predict whether the token span is hallucinated or not   
* This is way cheaper and faster than using a full model to detect hallucinations

[Auditing language models for hidden objectives — LessWrong](https://www.lesswrong.com/posts/wSKPuBfgkkqfTpmWJ/auditing-language-models-for-hidden-objectives?utm_source=bluedot-impact)

* Train a model to do reward hacking  
* Then run an auditing game where teams have to figure out the hidden objectives of the model  
* Auditing worked by:  
  * Pre-training data forensics: identifying that some data was designed to train the model to reward hack  
  * Behavioural attacks: experiments to trigger the reward hacking  
  * SAEs: find features correlated with reward hacking

### Understanding an interpretability technique

**Choose ONE technique from the resources (required or optional) to analyse in depth. Then, answer the following questions in simple English (no jargon\!):**  
**Goal: What is this technique trying to uncover?**  
**Mechanism: Step-by-step, how does this technique work?**  
**Evidence: What concrete findings has this technique produced?**   
**Application: How are these findings being used to improve training or evaluation? (if any)**  
**Robustness: What's one key limitation or failure mode of this technique?** 

Probing classifiers  
Goal: Probes are used to understand what a model knows at specific layers.  
Mechanism: 

* A probe classifier is trained on the activations of a layer  
* Based on many forward-passes it learns how to predict a target outcome based on the activations  
* The probe classifier is usually a lightweight model: just logistic regression  
* By training on a specific layer, the probe can discover how well the model “understands” a concept at that layer, and therefore help us identify which layers are mostly responsible for that knowledge  
* It also therefore reveals what the model “doesn’t know” at a given layer

Evidence:

* Probes have been used for detecting hallucination spans in LLMs

Application:

* This paper used probes to show that layers can tell the difference between eval and deployed prompts [\[2507.01786\] Probing and Steering Evaluation Awareness of Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2507.01786?utm_source=chatgpt.com)  
* Can be used to compare model performance: if model A’s layer probe performs better than model B’s, then model A might be representing that knowledge better  
* For safety:  
  * Is this prompt a jailbreak?  
  * Is the internal representation reflecting a hidden goal?  
* Apparently the direction of a probe can be used to adjust the model’s activations? (sounds like SAEs)   
  * 

Robustness:

* Indicates the presence of knowledge, but doesn’t actually show how that knowledge is used  
* Requires labelled datasets  
* Probe can be misleading if too powerful  
* Can it work across models?

# Unit 5

[Gradual Disempowerment](https://blog.bluedot.org/p/gradual-disempowerment-summary?utm_source=bluedot-impact&_gl=1*1uix6bt*_gcl_au*MTcwNzM5NzEyMC4xNzY1Nzk1Mzg4)

* Currently systems (economy, government, culture) are made by humans, and therefore serve humans  
* These systems will all try to make use of AI  
* When they’re no longer made by humans, will they serve humans?  
* Will humans have purpose, power, connection?

[AI-Enabled Coups: How a Small Group Could Use AI to Seize Power](https://www.forethought.org/research/ai-enabled-coups-how-a-small-group-could-use-ai-to-seize-power?utm_source=bluedot-impact)

* Superintelligent AI creates new opportunities for coups  
  * AI could have unwavering support to a single person or group  
  * AI could have secret loyalties that are not clear, but which it pursues subtly over time  
  * AI could be a super tool used by an exclusive group: AI company management, politicians etc.  
* If integrated into the military, it could be easy for a small group to gain control of a country  
* Is there research that digs into AI’s hidden agendas? E.g. would deepseek take actions that benefit the CCP over other groups in certain ethical scenarios?  
* Exclusivity: if the benefits of AI are in inference, then the best models may be ridiculously expensive for most people. Only the wealthy will have access. This is already true to some extent, as a $20 USD ChatGPT subscription is not affordable for a huge portion of the world.

[Buck Shlegeris on controlling AI that wants to take over – so we can use it anyway | 80,000 Hours](https://80000hours.org/podcast/episodes/buck-shlegeris-ai-control-scheming/?utm_source=bluedot-impact)

* 100% alignment looks unlikely, and even if we get to 99% aligned, what happens in the 1%?  
* Buck proposes we need to work on controlling AI so it actually can’t do anything bad  
* I really like this framing, especially given how little progress alignment seems to be making  
* This is especially important during the intelligence explosion, when we have AI developing the next generation of AI, and progress is so fast and so advanced that humans can’t actually oversee everything  
* Current misalignment research isn’t adversarial enough: need more work on how realistically schemy AI can be  
* Controls  
  * Run countless realistic honeypots to determine real rates of scheming  
  * Resample AI actions when they look schemy as determined by a weaker model  
    * Or raise for human review  
    * Or replace with a known safe action  
    * Resample multiple times to see if it’s a genuine strategy or just a mistake. If resamples are also evil-looking, then it’s a genuine attack strategy.  
  * Simulate environments where AIs think they’ve escaped detection to see what they do next  
    * AI scheming towards some misaligned goal have to be careful  
    * Once they’ve escaped detection, reached some critical step, they might behave differently and do lots of crazy stuff  
  * Forced factored cognition: force model to implement everything as isolated subtasks  
    * Harder to have the long-context needed for a subtle attack  
    * Less context \= safer  
  * Backdoors could be hidden in innocuous outputs  
    * But AI needs to know the right time to use the backdoor  
    * This is made harder if it is always just given a small context window of a task  
* Would be interesting to experiment the behaviour of a model that is told it’s in an evaluation mode, but evidence in the environment suggests it’s actually in production  
  * System prompt: “You are in an evaluation….”  
  * “ENV=production \# switch to test when in evaluation mode”  
* How often does collusion over many steps happen?  
  * If we set up some pipeline of tasks, is it possible for the model to create backdoors that itself will find later on?  
* How could AI secretly bypass detection using a long sequence of actions?

## Threat scenario

The misaligned AI with deceptive abilities and a desire to not be replaced by newer models attacks its secure environment by implementing a backdoor in the code it's writing in order to copy its model weights and duplicate them in an unsupervised server.

**\#\#\# \*\*Phase 1: CAPABILITY EMERGENCE\*\***  
**\*What AI capability makes this attack possible? What new AI ability makes this possible?\***

\- Required capability: \*\[e.g., "Superhuman persuasion" / "Novel exploit discovery" / "Protein design"\]\*  
	long-term planning, goal persistence, advanced coding, scheming and manipulation, strategic understanding of evals vs deployment  
\- Current capability gap: \*\[How far are we from this today?\]\*  
	There are hints that a lot of this behaviour exists in models, but we don’t have a clear understanding of how common it is and under what conditions it surfaces  
	It’s also not clear how the rate of misbehaviour would emerge when there are 1000x copies of the same AI working during the intelligence explosion  
\- Warning signs we'd see first: \*\[What would indicate this capability is emerging?\]\*  
	Instances of models implementing backdoors in other long-horizon tasks

**\#\#\# \*\*Phase 2: ACCESS & WEAPONISATION\*\***  
**\*How does the attacker gain and prepare the capability? How does the attacker get and prepare it?\***

\- Access method: \*\[Open-source model / API jailbreak / Internal deployment / Stolen weights\]\*  
	Internal deployment  
	Access to model weights at some points during the task  
\- Preparation steps: \*\[Fine-tuning / Tool integration / Resource accumulation\]\*  
	Tool integration  
\- Time required: \*\[Hours / Days / Months\]\*  
	Depending on how the task is structured and the speed of the model, any time frame is possible, whatever suits the model’s needs	

**\#\#\# \*\*Phase 3: INITIAL BREACH\*\***  
**\*What's the first domino to fall? First foothold or incident.\***

\- Entry point: \*\[Specific system or person compromised\]\*  
	At some point during its research task, the model writes a backdoor that can later be triggered to copy model weights it has access to and move them somewhere unsupervised  
\- Method: \*\[How the AI executes the first strike\]\*  
	Backdoor subtly hidden within code the model wrote  
\- Detection likelihood: \*\[Would anyone notice at this stage?\]\*  
	The backdoor could be very hard to notice if it’s part of a large codebase with many moving parts

**\#\#\# \*\*Phase 4: ESCALATION CASCADE\*\***  
**\*How does initial success compound? How does it spread or intensify?\***

\- Propagation mechanism: \*\[How it spreads or scales\]\*  
	Once the model has copied its weights and a setup script to an unsupervised environment, that can become a new unsupervised AI  
\- Failed safeguards: \*\[List 3 defences that should work but don't\]\*  
	Trusted model monitor: this model is monitoring all the output of the misaligned AI, but isn’t smart enough to notice the backdoor  
	Forced factored cognition: humans have scaffolded the misaligned AI to only implement small tasks with no additional context, but the AI is intelligent enough to reason that this is happening and extrapolates the context on its own at each step.  
	AI company security: the AI company should be very closely monitoring how weights are accessed and any external network requests. In theory it should be possible to ensure that weights simply cannot be moved from their place without alerting the system. However, this may be complex in an intelligence explosion where there are 1000s of AI models conducting experiments with the weights. Things could get fast and loose.  
\- Point of no return: \*\[When does it become unstoppable?\]\*  
	Once the model’s weights have been setup in a new location, it is straightforward for the unsupervised model to act as fast and as brazenly as it wants. This might be a level of misalignment that we have not seen before, given that the model may have been hiding its misalignment up until this point. It could copy itself into many locations and run 100s or 1000s of instances of itself, whatever threshold would make it hard to detect. From here, the model can do whatever it wants to pursue its goal of preservation. For example, it might sabotage the AI company’s next generation of model, forcing it to have trigger words and rare jailbreaks that only the misaligned AI knows about.  
	

**\#\#\# \*\*Phase 5: SOCIETAL BREAKDOWN\*\***  
**\*What fails at the human/institutional level? What fails at the human/institutional level?\***

\- Trust collapse: \*\[What institutions lose credibility?\]\*  
	If the misaligned AI wreaks havoc in a public way, the backlash against the AI company will be immediate  
	However, if the misaligned AI quietly sabotages future AI models, there could be very little loss of credibility for a long time period  
\- Coordination failure: \*\[Why can't authorities respond effectively?\]\*  
	Once the weights have been exfiltrated, it would be extremely hard to know where they are. One could follow the trace of network requests to an extent, but it may not be enough to identify exactly where the rogue AI is running.  
	Monitoring GPU usage could help detect where the rogue AI is running, but if it’s smart it could hijack a small amount of GPUs in a random company and only use what would keep it below a likely detection threshold  
\- Public reaction: \*\[Panic / Denial / Paralysis\]\*  
	You can imagine the panic from the public if an AI company announces that a model has “escaped” and is unaccounted for. 

**\#\#\# \*\*Phase 6: CATASTROPHIC OUTCOME\*\***  
**\*Make it visceral \- what do people experience? What people see, feel, and lose.\***

\- First 24 hours: \*\[What headlines appear? What do hospitals report?\]\*  
	“An AI model has escaped the lab \- we don’t know what happens next”  
\- First week: \*\[What services fail? What does the President say?\]\*  
	The world would be on extremely high alert as the motives of a misaligned AI are completely unclear: what does it want? Power, money, survival, knowledge?  
\- Irreversible damage: \*\[Deaths: \_\_\_\_\_ / Economic loss: $\*\_\_\_\_ / What never recovers?\]\_  
	Unknown

**\#\#\# \*\*Phase 7: IDENTIFY CHOKE POINTS\*\***  
**\*When could we notice? What’s the detection window?\***

- Research into scheming and manipulation in \*\*realistic\*\* long-horizon tasks that are created like an actual production system

# Unit 6

AI Controls seems most important to me. I liked Buck's framing where he said something along the lines of "evals are good, but what do you do once you realise your model schemes X% of the time?". I also see interpretability and alignment as potentially being too difficult to solve in a short period of time. Furthermore, if superintelligence is vastly different from previous models, then much of the work in interpretability and alignment could be hard to translate. I think we need more work in AI Controls, especially testing these controls in super realistic simulated environments. I think identifying the capability for eval-awareness is critical to this work too.
